draft.introduction: Euclid’s Elements (c. 300 BCE) begins with definitions, postulates, and common notions from which all of plane geometry is derived. It is the most influential textbook in the history of mathematics and the model of deductive reasoning.

translation.text: A point is that which has no part. A line is breadthless length. A straight line is a line which lies evenly with the points on itself. A surface is that which has length and breadth only. Let the following be postulated: to draw a straight line from any point to any point; to produce a finite straight line continuously in a straight line; to describe a circle with any center and distance.
translation.original_text: Σημεῖόν ἐστιν οὗ μέρος οὐθέν · γραμμὴ δὲ μῆκος ἀπλατές

translator_notes.note: The fifth postulate (the parallel postulate) states that non-parallel lines must eventually meet. Attempts to prove it from the other four postulates failed for two millennia, until the 19th century when its denial produced non-Euclidean geometries—which proved essential to Einstein’s general relativity.
